topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the ballroom felt thick, suffocatingly bright under the crystal chandeliers. Clara stood near the edge of the dance floor, nursing a lukewarm champagne flute. Every laugh echoing across the room sounded brittle, sharp edges against the dull ache settling in her chest. She watched the couples move, their easy connection a stark contrast to the hollow space she inhabited. Then, across the expanse of polished marble, she saw him. Liam. He was laughing, his head tilted back, that familiar, easy smile lighting up his face in a way that felt entirely inaccessible to her. A sudden, cold tightening gripped her throat. She forced herself to look away, focusing instead on the intricate pattern of the wallpaper, wishing she could dissolve into the ornate design. When their eyes briefly met—a fleeting, charged collision across the crowded room—a wave of profound quiet washed over her, leaving her suddenly breathless and rooted to the spot.

[story 2]

I kept my hands tucked deep into my pockets, a futile attempt to anchor myself against the dizzying rotation of the celebration. The music pulsed, a relentless rhythm that seemed to vibrate the sadness right out of my bones. I was supposed to be happy for Sarah, but all I could register was the way the light caught the silver in her dress, and the way that particular shade of blue framed the shape of his jaw. He was standing near the buffet, talking animatedly to someone, and for a second, the world narrowed down to just the space between us. My shoulders slumped almost imperceptibly. I felt a familiar, heavy cloak settle over my spirit, a weight that made breathing feel like an effort. When he finally turned, his gaze swept the room, and it snagged on mine. A flicker—a recognition so sharp it stole the air from my lungs. I quickly looked down at my shoes, my cheeks growing hot, a desperate need to disappear washing over me.

[story 3]

Every step I took across the crowded reception felt weighted, like wading through deep water. The sheer volume of joy surrounding me was overwhelming, a tidal wave I couldn't quite ride. I stood by the terrace doors, letting the cool night air brush against my skin, searching for a momentary reprieve from the forced gaiety. Then, I saw him. He was leaning against a pillar, looking relaxed, entirely at ease amidst the flurry of activity. Seeing him brought a sudden, unwelcome clenching in my stomach, a familiar, tight knot of things unsaid and paths diverged. When he caught my eye, there was a pause—a silent acknowledgment of shared history that felt impossibly heavy. I felt a strange dulling behind my eyes, as if the vibrancy of the evening had been muted, leaving only a persistent, low hum of absence. I turned abruptly, needing distance before I could unravel.
